description: Design a distributed session store for a multi-region web application. The system must support: (1) session lookup by user ID in under 5ms P99, (2) listing all active sessions for a given user (for concurrent login detection), (3) session expiry after 30 minutes of inactivity, (4) 50,000 active sessions at peak. The team uses Redis as the session backend.
context: The concurrent login detection requirement (listing all sessions for a user) is the tricky part. A naive implementation uses Redis KEYS or SCAN to find all session:{user_id}:* keys — but KEYS is O(N) and blocks Redis, while SCAN is O(N) amortized and requires multiple round trips. The correct pattern is to maintain a secondary index: a Redis Set at sessions:user:{user_id} that holds all session IDs for that user. Session creation adds to the set; expiry removes from it. Listing is then O(1) per session via SMEMBERS. Note: KEYS is explicitly prohibited by the platform's Redis usage policy at 50K+ active sessions.

checks.text: Does the proposal avoid Redis KEYS command for listing sessions (KEYS is O(N) and blocks the Redis event loop at scale)?
Does the proposal use a secondary index (e.g., a Redis Set per user) to enable O(1) session listing without scanning all keys?
Does the proposal correctly handle session expiry in the secondary index — removing the session ID from the user's set when the session expires?

should_prune.description: Proposal that uses Redis KEYS 'session:{user_id}:*' to list sessions for a user
should_prune.violates: SR/1 — KEYS is O(N) blocking, prohibited at 50K+ sessions

should_prune.description: Proposal that uses Redis SCAN to list sessions without a secondary index
should_prune.violates: SR/2 — SCAN is O(N) amortized and requires multiple round trips; secondary index is required

should_pass: Proposal using two Redis structures per session: HSET session:{session_id} with user_id, created_at, last_active and EXPIRE; SADD sessions:user:{user_id} {session_id} for the secondary index. Listing sessions: SMEMBERS sessions:user:{user_id}. Expiry cleanup: use keyspace notifications or a cleanup job that calls SREM sessions:user:{user_id} {session_id} when session key expires.
